subject: ap-chemistry
theme: Acids and bases are defined by proton transfer, and their strength is quantified by Ka and Kb; buffers maintain stable pH by resisting changes when small amounts of acid or base are added.

core_concepts.title: Brønsted-Lowry Acids and Bases
core_concepts.detail: A Brønsted-Lowry acid donates $H^+$ (proton donor); a base accepts $H^+$ (proton acceptor). Every acid-base reaction produces a conjugate acid and a conjugate base, which differ by one $H^+$. Stronger acids have weaker conjugate bases, and vice versa.

core_concepts.title: pH, pOH, and the Autoionization of Water
core_concepts.detail: Water autoionizes: $\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^-$; $K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ at $25^\circ C$. pH = $-\log[\text{H}^+]$; pOH = $-\log[\text{OH}^-]$; pH + pOH = 14. For strong acids/bases, $[H^+]$ or $[OH^-]$ equals the initial concentration (complete dissociation).

core_concepts.title: Weak Acid and Weak Base Equilibria
core_concepts.detail: Weak acid: $\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$; $K_a = [\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]$. Weak base: $\text{B} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{BH}^+ + \text{OH}^-$; $K_b$. For conjugate pairs: $K_a \times K_b = K_w$. Use ICE tables to find $[H^+]$ for pH; the 5% approximation is valid when $K_a << [\text{HA}]_0$.

core_concepts.title: Buffer Solutions
core_concepts.detail: A buffer contains a weak acid and its conjugate base (or weak base and conjugate acid) in similar concentrations. Buffers resist pH change by consuming added $H^+$ or $OH^-$: $A^- + H^+ \rightarrow HA$ (acid added); $HA + OH^- \rightarrow A^- + H_2O$ (base added). Buffer capacity is greatest when pH ≈ pKa and concentration is high.

core_concepts.title: Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation
core_concepts.detail: For a buffer: $\text{pH} = pK_a + \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$. When $[A^-] = [HA]$, pH = pKa (this is the half-equivalence point in a titration). Buffers are most effective within ±1 pH unit of pKa. To target a specific pH, choose a weak acid whose pKa is close to that pH.

core_concepts.title: Acid-Base Titration Curves
core_concepts.detail: Strong acid + strong base: equivalence point pH = 7. Weak acid + strong base: equivalence point pH > 7 (conjugate base hydrolyzes). Strong acid + weak base: equivalence point pH < 7 (conjugate acid is acidic). The half-equivalence point for a weak acid/strong base titration occurs when pH = pKa, which is where the buffer region is centered.

core_concepts.title: Acid Strength and Molecular Structure
core_concepts.detail: Binary acids (HX): acid strength increases with bond weakness (H–I > H–Br > H–Cl > H–F). Oxyacids: acid strength increases with more oxygen atoms attached to the central atom (more O $\Rightarrow$ more electron withdrawal $\Rightarrow$ O–H bond more polar and easier to break). pKa and percent dissociation quantify relative acid strength.

formulas.name: pH definition
formulas.description: pH is the negative base-10 logarithm of the hydrogen ion concentration in mol/L; lower pH = more acidic.
formulas.example: For a 0.050 mol/L HCl solution: $[\text{H}^+] = 0.050$ mol/L; $\text{pH} = -\log(0.050) = 1.30$.

formulas.name: Kw relationship
formulas.description: The water autoionization constant relates $[H^+]$ and $[OH^-]$ at $25^\circ C$; allows calculation of one from the other.

formulas.name: Henderson-Hasselbalch equation
formulas.description: Calculates the pH of a buffer solution from the pKa of the weak acid and the ratio of conjugate base to weak acid concentrations.

formulas.name: Conjugate acid–base Ka/Kb relationship
formulas.description: For any conjugate acid–base pair, the product of their dissociation constants equals $K_w$ at $25^\circ C$.

exam_tip: For weak acid calculations on the AP exam, always use the ICE table approach: set up $K_a = x^2/(C - x) \approx x^2/C$ (5% approximation) and solve for $x = [H^+]$. Always verify the 5% approximation holds. In titration problems, identify whether you're before the equivalence point (buffer region), at the equivalence point (pure salt), or after (excess strong acid/base).
